speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing Joseph Carver in London, England. Joe, you were you saying that you had just been interviewed. What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had the sandwiches. We had the sandwiches, which the inspector gave to my mother. So, when nobody was looking, and we ate these sandwiches among ourselves. There were some other people there as well-- Jewish people-- and we shared some of the sandwiches with them because they were in similar circumstances. They had nothing to eat. At around about late in the afternoon, they took us out in cars, and they took us to the railway station. The railway station in Leipzig was one of the largest in Europe. It had something like 42 or 48 platforms. It was catering for the international fairs, which were held twice a year. The town itself was known for publishing, book printing, and the fur trade, which was predominantly in Jewish hands. My father, besides having a textile agency, also used to deal in ready-made fur coats. And he had a large clientele of theatrical people-- people who were in the theatrical professional, like singers, and actresses, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and some of their relatives. Like, they used to take the coats from my father, and take it home to their relatives, and sell it on his behalf because Jews were not allowed to trade. And Germans were not allowed to buy anything from Jews. So this was a sort of a clandestine operation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we were loaded on the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were not wagons. They were proper trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had you been told you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not told our destination. So we went into the trains, and in each compartment was a German guard. So we had nothing to carry, but other people who have had different gestapos-- people who came to see them-- some had suitcases, some had holdalls, some had different kinds of bags with whatever they had. I don't know. Whatever they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the German population at the railway station said, who are these people? What are they doing? Why are they being [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] pushed around by the SS that way? They said, oh, these are all Jews. They are all criminals. And they go where they come from. And we still didn't know where we were going. Where did you think you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in your mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing whatsoever. Now, when we were about halfway of the journey, my father, who frequently went to Poland, looked out and he said, yes, we are heading for Poland. It was familiar territory, and he knew we were going in the direction of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, how many of you were in the compartment with the German guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the compartment, I would think there was my father, my mother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four-- us four boys. That was six. And there was another Jewish couple. Eight. Eight people. And the German guard was standing, and then he went out, and he came back, because they were not supposed to sit with Jews and there was only four seats on each side. So this was eight in the compartment. But he kept a watchful eye from outside. As you know, a train, there's a corridor in the train, and when you sort of walk from one place to the other. And he was standing outside, keeping an eye on us, and also on the other compartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: So certain people wanted to go to the toilet. They said, no, you're not allowed to use a toilet. You can drink your Jewish urine. You're not allowed to go to the toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when it was getting dark-- it was already night-- the train stopped and we were taken over by German border guards. They were called Grenzschutz-- border guards. They were young hooligans. They were between 25 to 35 years old. They had whips in their hand. They wear-- they wore black uniforms, which meant they were SS units. And they beat us. I was beaten, my mother, my father-- all the Jewish people along there were beaten. And they told us to run. So the elderly people, they fell on the rails. And of course, some had concussion on their legs and bruises. They had to get up. And whatever they were carrying was really whipped out, with the whips, from their hands. So really, all their property was on German soil. And this went on for about half an hour, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till we went on Polish soil. Once we were on Polish soil, we were on our own and they withdrew. It's only later on we found out that the fifth [? columns, ?] the ethnic Germans in Poland, made it very easy for them. Conveniently, there were no Polish border guards at the time when the deportation took place. Really, in fact, what they done is they shoved us on Polish territory without the Poles knowing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were on Polish territory and we saw, in the distance, a house with a light. And we marched on it. And a man came out with a shot gun and he said, in Polish, who are you? What do you want? Where do you come from? So a few people were Polish speaking. They explained to them that they were Polish citizens. They were just expelled from Germany by the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the man said, you are not allowed to come any nearer. You have got to stay where you are. I am going to get in touch with the authority.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he couldn't get in touch with the authority because the telephone lines were cut. So he was immobilized. In fact, he had to come down, with his shot gun, and told us that we should stay in one position, not go any further. He will have to walk to the next village, try to get a horse to make contact with the authorities in Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's what he did. But in the mean time, certain people of our group also went to that little place where the Pole was going. And luckily, there were the telephone connections. They phoned the Jewish authorities in Warsaw and in Lodz what had happened. And help quickly arrived within a few hours, and everything was properly organized. My father, whose brothers were living in Lodz-- of course, his family, his sisters, his brothers, and the rest of all the clan were living in Lodz. We got a railway ticket. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to change German Marks for Polish Zlotys, buy a railway ticket, and come to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had taken money with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a few-- some currency, which wasn't taken from him, luckily. That's how we managed to come. But otherwise, he had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the other people go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other people, they were at the mercy of the Jewish organizations who, by then, came and tried to repatriate everybody wherever they had family. Some people went to Lemberg, like Lwow, Krakow, Warsaw, some other small, little places. But in order to go to the small, little places, they had to go to the big towns first. They had to be repatriated. As we were already in Silesia, it wasn't very far from people who came from the Silesia region to go to Katowice, which was a big town. It was a big town and had a large, Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you went to your uncle's?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Lodz. So when we came out from the railway station, they had the carriages. They didn't have motorcars in those days, only a few motorcars in Poland. It was mainly a horse and carriage. And we gave the destination, which was Gdanska 28.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in those days, most of the-- what are they called in English, these-- fellow who drive-- coach drivers. Most of them were Jewish, and while they were driving us to our destination, they said, who has done this to you? Hitler? May he burn in hell. May he never come to our place. May he never come to Poland. So little did we know that he would follow us very soon. And of course, there was great jubilation when we arrived. And my aunts and my uncles, they were already well informed in advance. So we stayed with my uncle overnight. The following morning, we were billeted. My brothers went to other cousins, other uncles. And-- so we were distributed, between one family to the other family. And who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was staying with my father, mother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we were staying with my uncle, who had a son who was the same age as myself. After one week, my uncle found us accommodation. We moved out and we had a two and half room apartment in the center of town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we got new furniture, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who paid for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncles. My father's brothers. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was given tuition in the Polish language. And after three months, I joined my cousin in his school, which was the Jewish high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who taught you the Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I talk Polish, yes. I speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who taught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Professional teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish teachers. Professionals, yes. I first started to know all the animals in Polish, and the main things about, like, bread, water, butter, things like this. And sir and lady, and-- Poles have different name for unmarried woman, for a married woman, and for young girl. It's, grammatically, really very funny. But anyway, I somehow mastered it. When one is young and a child, it comes very easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father working at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. After that, my father went to join his brothers in factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So while we were staying with our family, which was a very, very large-- there were so many cousins. I often wonder how big our clan was. One of my father's uncles, he had 16 children. Some of them were already grown up and they are children of their own, like six, seven, [INAUDIBLE]. One, I think, had eight. So there were very large families in those days. And we made our rounds, either on the tram car or we walked. As I said before, cars, in Poland, at that time, were very scarce. There wasn't any traffic. One would see, now and again, a few cars, but that was about all. According to the standard-- the German standard, I would say Poland was very primitive. Very, very primitive.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what sort of ways was it primitive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had flushing toilets. Some places, they had flushing toilets, some not. Where we had, luckily, it was a modern house, built in 1936. So we had flushing toilets there, but we had no central heating. So our-- our heating was done by coal fires as they have in England. Through the chimney, the smoke was going, just like here. And coal was obtainable. The Poles-- coal-- Polish coal merchants, they usually carried the coal out. People lived in apartment blocks. First, second, and third story. We lived, at the time, on the third floor, I think. If I can remember-- yes, third floor. And the coal man came up and I think he was carrying 100 weight, which was quite heavy. And little did I know that I would be carrying also 100 weight in Auschwitz a year after that. No, not-- not a year after that. That was in 1944. Five years after that, because I was already older then. But that's how it happened. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life in Poland before the war was fairly primitive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your school like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: School was all right. We had wash basins and-- and toilet facilities. It was a Jewish school, but I sometimes went to Polish schools, just, like, to see what they had in comparison to ours. Theirs were really very primitive. Classrooms and-- they were not as nice as ours. They just had the basics, and our classrooms were more modern, more up to date. They compared favorably to the Jewish school in Germany. So this was really good. Food was available in Poland, plentiful, but there was a lot of poverty. Such a lot of poverty which we have never seen. We saw children half starved, Jewish children without clothing, bare feet. And we, who were refugees ourselves, had pity on these people and gave them some of our clothing. And we didn't have much, but we couldn't see anyone, in winter, being without socks, or torn trousers, or even overcoats. So whatever we had, we gave it to them, some of the children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some people-- some Jewish people, who had jobs or they owned business, were all right. Some looked after their families, others sort of didn't bother. They were very, very selfish. Now, my father's family was very, very clannish. They looked after each other, and I think this was really a blessing that, when we came, we had a home to go to, we had people who cared after us, people who took an interest in us. And that was really very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: The non-Jewish population was very antisemitic. I would say, and that is, in all fairness, without any prejudice, that the Polish antisemitism was much stronger before the war than what we experienced in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that manifest itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: It manifested itself that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they hated Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When you went into an official [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] office of the government, like a tax office-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a large population of Jews were Hasidic, and they always wore their skull cap. They would take off their hat, or their cap, and they would still have the skull cap covering their head. And when the Poles saw that, they used to go to them and they used to have kind of [? tantrums. ?] They used to knock them on the head and they said, oi, Jew man, this is a government office. Respect for the government. Take your hat off. And they knew that these Jewish people would never really take their hat off. But there was no alternative, and they took it off. That's how antisemitic they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you, personally, experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: I experienced, when I went to school, I was beaten up by Polish boys, just like in Germany also. Jew boy, Jew pig. And three or four people would fight me, and I was one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the first day, I had to run in order to evade them. Although they caught up with me and one or two really hit me. But I managed to hit one of them, knock him on the floor. I sort of kicked him in the groin, and he fell, and I ran. So the following day, my brother, who worked in the vicinity, used to be my guard. And when he came, nobody dared to attack us because he just took one of them, and he was shaking him, and he was dancing around, and he couldn't catch his breath. So he said to the others, this fellow is a strong Jew. Better keep away. So it was endemic in the population. The Poles who worked for Jews, although they were paid for doing the job because jobs were very scarce, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] still had a hatred. They were washing the clothes, the laundry. The Jewish woman would pay them, would give them bread, would give them a meal, would give them tea.

speaker: interviewee
text: They would say, thank you very much, get their money, and then they would go out, and would say, bloody Jews. The day will come when they will work for us. That's what they used to say. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My uncle had a servant, Polish girl. She must have been about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in her late twenties. And she was cooking and cleaning. And of course, my cousin and his sister were not big eaters. They would sort of nibble, have a little meal and just look at it. And she would say, oh, such good food you are wasting. Well, you wait till Hitler comes. Then you will have nothing. Look at your cousin. Look at all these poor Jews who came from Germany. They got nothing. And I often wondered what she was saying. They had nothing. Till I found out, not far from where they were living-- on the same street, but a little bit further down-- there was a disused factory where about 100 Jewish families, deported from Germany, were living. And we went to see them. They were sleeping on the floors. They had no beds. People were giving them mattresses and bedding and pillowcases, and food, of course, clothing as well. And they were living just like Gypsies. They had no job. The Jewish organization in Lodz, which was like a relief organization, gave them maybe a few shillings a week to maintain themselves. Food was given. They had, like, soup kitchens, bread. On a Shabbat, they had a proper meal. I think they had wine because you cannot have a Shabbat meal without wine for the benediction. So there was always wine on their table. And I must say that the Jewish population, who was poor themselves, they were very benevolent, and they felt that these poor refugees, who were kicked out from their homes, living like gypsies in a disused factory, really deserved a little bit better. So they were looked after. Many years later, here in England, I came across [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people, men and women, who actually were in Lodz at the time. They didn't know me and I didn't know them. And I thought it was very fascinating.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked them, this happened in 1939, as far as I can remember. They said, oh, yes. So I said, well, how did you manage to get out and come to England? Because they came to England before the war. So they told me that they were told by the Jewish organization in Poland, through the Jewish organization that was left in Germany-- because only Polish Jews were deported, but German Jews were still living in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the papers have arrived for them to go to England. All they had to do come back to Germany and get that paper permit in order to go to England via Holland. So some of these people, who were children at the time, went to Germany, obtained the relative documents-- the permits-- to leave Germany, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went to Holland-- from Germany to Holland-- and from Holland to England. That's how they reached England before the war. But this was not something that you were aware of at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't aware at all. We had no inkling. This was all known to me afterwards, when I was already in England. I had no knowledge whatsoever. Because we were deported as a family unit, and we stayed together as a family. So we had no idea, whatsoever, that this was happening. Now the people who were living in this factory had some contacts with the Zionist organization in Germany. And from Germany, they got in touch with the Zionist organization in Poland. And my brother, who was in the Habonim movement, got to know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that arrangements were made for him to leave for Israel, which was Palestine at the time. And he told my father-- that was in July 1939-- that he is going with a contingent of 10 boys from Warsaw-- they're going to Warsaw, from Warsaw, they will go to Romania. And in Romania, they will get a ship which will take them to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course my mother and my father was very disturbed. My mother said, you are my only-- oldest son, you know, and without you, how will we manage? My father was a little bit more far sighted, and he said, yes, I think this is the best thing to do, to leave. So my brother left.
